content:
# Genesis Chapter 6 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 4r–4v | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_006_page_001.jpg, GEN_006_page_002.jpg
**Additional Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX | **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0008

---

**1** When human beings began to multiply across the face of the ground and daughters were born to them, **2** the sons of God saw that the daughters of men were beautiful — and they took any of them they wanted.

**3** Then the LORD said: "My spirit will not contend with humanity forever — for they are flesh. Their days will be one hundred and twenty years."

**4** The Nephilim were on the earth in those days, and also afterward — whenever the sons of God came in to the daughters of men and they bore children to them. These were the mighty ones of old, the men of renown.

---

**5** The LORD saw that the wickedness of humanity was great on the earth — and that every inclination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil, all the time.

**6** And the LORD regretted that he had made humanity on the earth. It grieved him to his heart.

**7** The LORD said: "I will wipe out humanity, whom I have created, from the face of the ground — human beings together with animals, crawling creatures, and the birds of the sky — for I regret that I made them."

---

**8** But Noah found favor in the eyes of the LORD.

---

**9** This is the account of Noah.

Noah was a righteous man. He was blameless among the people of his time. Noah walked with God.

**10** And Noah fathered three sons: Shem, Ham, and Japheth.

**11** Now the earth was corrupt before God's face, and the earth was filled with violence.

**12** God saw the earth — and it was corrupt. All flesh had corrupted their way on the earth.

**13** God said to Noah: "The end of all flesh has come before me, for the earth is filled with violence because of them. I am going to destroy them together with the earth.

**14** "Build yourself an ark — of cypress wood. Make rooms inside it and cover it with pitch inside and out.

**15** "This is how you are to build it: the ark is to be three hundred cubits long, fifty cubits wide, and thirty cubits high. **16** You are to make a roof for it, and finish it to within a cubit from the top. Set the entrance in its side. Make a lower deck, a second deck, and a third deck.

**17** "I am bringing the floodwaters over the earth to destroy all flesh under the sky that has the breath of life in it. Everything on the earth will die.

**18** "But I will establish my covenant with you — and you will enter the ark: you, and your sons, and your wife, and your sons' wives with you.

**19** "From every living creature, from all flesh, you are to bring two of every kind into the ark, to keep them alive with you. They are to be male and female — **20** birds according to their kinds, animals according to their kinds, and every creature that crawls on the ground according to its kind. Two of every kind will come to you, to be kept alive.

**21** "And take for yourself every kind of food that is eaten and store it away. It will serve as food for you and for them."

**22** And Noah did everything — exactly as God commanded him. He did it.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### On Genesis 6 as a whole — the pivot

Genesis 6 is the most theologically catastrophic chapter in the creation narrative. The God who stood over the finished world and declared it "very good" (טוֹב מְאֹד, *tov meod*, 1:31) now looks at that same world and regrets making it. The chapter is structured as a descent: from the mysterious to the cosmic to the devastating — interrupted by a single verse of grace — then a pivot into the technical preparation for survival.

There are three movements: the narrative of cosmic disruption (verses 1–4), the divine diagnosis and decision (verses 5–7), and the commissioning of Noah (verses 9–22). The hinge is verse 8 — one sentence, seven Hebrew words — standing alone between the announcement of destruction and the story of the one man who will survive it.

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 4r–4v, photographed by the West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Three-column format with full Tiberian Masoretic vocalization and cantillation. Additional reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana; Dead Sea Scrolls, 4QGen (4Q7) and related fragments.*

---

### Verses 1–4 — The sons of God and the daughters of men: what is this?

This passage is one of the strangest in all of Scripture, and the Hebrew offers no simple resolution. Two terms require confrontation:

**בְּנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים (benei ha'elohim)** — "the sons of God." In the ancient Near East, this phrase in cognate languages refers to divine beings, members of the heavenly court, supernatural entities. The same construction appears in Job 1:6 and 2:1, where the "sons of God" (בְּנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים) come before the LORD and Satan is among them — they are clearly heavenly beings, not humans. In Psalm 82, God stands in the divine assembly and judges the elohim, the heavenly beings who have been ruling the nations corruptly. The phrase has a consistent meaning in its textual world: these are not simply men of godly lineage. They are beings from the divine realm.

The ancient interpretive tradition agrees. The Book of Enoch (1 Enoch 6–11), composed before the Common Era, tells the story of these "Watchers" — divine beings who descended and "took" human women, producing giants. This is the interpretive tradition that the New Testament authors knew: Jude 1:6 and 2 Peter 2:4 both appear to allude to the fall of angels who "did not keep their own domain." The LXX Vaticanus renders "sons of God" here as **οἱ ἄγγελοι τοῦ θεοῦ** — "the angels of God" — making the supernatural reading explicit. The Alexandrian tradition did not hesitate.

A later interpretation, adopted by some rabbinic authorities and by many patristic writers who were uncomfortable with the mythological reading, identified "sons of God" as "sons of the rulers" — the noble or powerful class of men. On this reading, the passage describes aristocratic polygamous exploitation of women below their social class. But this reading requires the phrase בְּנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים to mean something it never means elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible.

My translation preserves "sons of God" without domesticating it. The phrase is eerie. It should remain eerie. The ancient world knew what it meant and did not need it explained. We should feel its strangeness.

**הַנְּפִלִים (hannephilim)** — "the Nephilim." The etymology is disputed but likely derives from נָפַל (nafal), "to fall." The Nephilim may be "the fallen ones," "those who fell from above," or simply "the ones who cause others to fall." Numbers 13:33 will use the word again, where the Israelite spies see the Anakim and say "we were in our own sight as grasshoppers" — the Nephilim are there, impossibly large. The tradition of giants, offspring of divine-human union, is ancient and widespread in the ancient Near East. The text presents them as historical — they were on the earth "in those days, and also afterward" — connecting the pre-flood world to the world the first readers knew.

The structure of verse 4 is careful: the Nephilim were there *before* and *after* the sons of God came to human women. The second category — the "mighty ones of old, the men of renown" (הַגִּבֹּרִים אֲשֶׁר מֵעוֹלָם אַנְשֵׁי הַשֵּׁם, *hagibbborim asher me'olam anshei hashem*) — are the offspring of the divine-human unions. These are the heroes of legend, the culture-heroes, the demigods of every ancient mythology. Genesis is acknowledging them. It is not endorsing their worship. It is placing them inside a larger story.

The DSS material (4QGen) does not substantially alter the consonantal text here, but the Book of Giants (4Q531–4Q533) found at Qumran expands this story at great length, confirming that the Qumran community read these verses as referring to supernatural unions and their monstrous offspring.

---

### Verse 3 — "My spirit will not contend with humanity forever"

The Hebrew of this verse is among the most difficult in the chapter: לֹא־יָדוֹן רוּחִי בָאָדָם לְעֹלָם בְּשַׁגַּם הוּא בָשָׂר (*lo-yadon ruchi va'adam le'olam beshagam hu basar*).

**יָדוֹן (yadon)** — the verb is hapax legomenon in Hebrew: it appears only here. The root is uncertain. Possibilities include:
- From דִּין (din), "to judge/contend/plead" — "my spirit will not judge/contend with humanity"
- From נָדַן (nadan), "to remain/stay/abide" — "my spirit will not remain/abide in humanity"
- A scribal variant of נָדוֹן (nadon)

Most modern translations follow the "contend/strive" rendering. The ancient versions are divided: the LXX reads **οὐ μὴ καταμείνῃ τὸ πνεῦμά μου ἐν τοῖς ἀνθρώποις τούτοις εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα** — "my spirit shall not remain in these people forever" — favoring the "abide/remain" reading. The Vulgate reads *non permanebit* — "shall not remain." The Targum reads "shall not contend."

**בְּשַׁגַּם הוּא בָשָׂר (beshagam hu basar)** — "because/in that he is flesh." The particle בְּשַׁגַּם (beshagam) is also unusual — a compound of בְּ (in/because), שֶׁ (that/which), and גַּם (also/indeed). The sense is "because/in that/since indeed he is flesh." Flesh (בָּשָׂר, *basar*) here means mortal creaturely existence — not sinfulness per se, but limitation, embodied finitude. The divine spirit and human flesh are in tension; the spirit cannot contend or remain without limit.

**מֵאָה וְעֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה (meah ve'esrim shanah)** — "one hundred and twenty years." What does this limit refer to? Two main readings:
1. A new limit on individual human lifespan — from this point forward, no human will live beyond 120 years. But this is complicated by the fact that the patriarchs after the flood in Genesis 11 continue to live for hundreds of years.
2. A countdown to the flood — God is announcing 120 years until judgment falls, giving humanity a window to repent or to prepare. This reading fits the narrative better and is favored by many ancient interpreters, including the LXX tradition and 1 Peter 3:20's reference to God "waiting patiently" in the days of Noah.

I render the verse as a divine declaration without resolving the ambiguity: "Their days will be one hundred and twenty years." The translation lets the reader sit with both possibilities.

---

### Verse 5 — "every inclination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil, all the time"

The Hebrew accumulates with an insistence that English struggles to match:

כִּי רַב רָעַת הָאָדָם בָּאָרֶץ וְכָל־יֵצֶר מַחְשְׁבֹת לִבּוֹ רַק רַע כָּל־הַיּוֹם

(*ki rav ra'at ha'adam ba'arets, vekhol-yetser machshevot libbo raq ra kol-hayyom*)

**רַב (rav)** — "great/much/abundant." Wickedness was not merely present. It was *abundant*.

**יֵצֶר (yetser)** — "inclination/impulse/formation." The word comes from the verb יָצַר (yatsar), "to form" — the same root used of God forming the human from the dust (2:7) and forming the animals (2:19). The *yetser* is what has been formed in a person, the inner shape of their will and imagination. This word will become central to later Jewish theology — the *yetser hara* (evil inclination) and *yetser hatov* (good inclination) as the two forces in every human heart. Here, in its earliest use, there is only one inclination. Only evil. All the time.

**רַק רָע (raq ra)** — "only evil." The adverb רַק (raq) is exclusionary and absolute: *nothing but*, *solely*, *only*. Not mostly evil. Not evil on balance. Only. There is no remainder of good.

**כָּל־הַיּוֹם (kol-hayyom)** — "all the day/all the time." Not sometimes. Not usually. Every day, continuously, without exception.

The verse is a total indictment. The narrator is not describing one person or one nation. The narrator is describing the condition of humanity (הָאָדָם, *ha'adam* — the human being, collective) as a species. The diagnostic is absolute before the sentence is pronounced.

The LXX Vaticanus renders: **ὅτι πᾶς ἄνθρωπος διανοεῖται ἐπιμελῶς ἐπὶ τὰ πονηρὰ ἐκ νεότητος** — "for every human being diligently meditates on evil things from youth." The LXX adds "from youth" (ἐκ νεότητος) — a phrase that will appear in Genesis 8:21 in the MT, after the flood, where God's conclusion is: "the inclination of the human heart is evil from youth." The LXX appears to anticipate that phrase, harmonizing forward. The MT holds the two occurrences apart: before the flood the diagnosis, after the flood the recognition. The movement matters.

---

### Verse 6 — God's grief: the most theologically shattering sentence in Genesis

וַיִּנָּחֶם יְהוָה כִּי־עָשָׂה אֶת־הָאָדָם בָּאָרֶץ וַיִּתְעַצֵּב אֶל־לִבּוֹ

(*vayyinnachem YHWH ki-asah et-ha'adam ba'arets vayyit'atsev el-libbo*)

**וַיִּנָּחֶם (vayyinnachem)** — "and he regretted/relented/was grieved." The root נָחַם (nacham) is the most contested word in this verse and perhaps in this chapter. It carries a semantic range that no single English word captures:

1. **Regret** — looking back on a decision and wishing it had not been made.
2. **Relent** — changing a course of action, turning from a stated or intended path.
3. **Be comforted** — the same root is used for being consoled in grief (its other primary meaning).

The same root is used in Noah's naming in 5:29 — Lamech hoped his son would "comfort/relieve" (נִחַם, *nicham*) humanity from the curse. The word that named Noah now describes God's anguish over what humanity has become.

This verse presents a genuine theological crisis. If God regrets, does God make mistakes? Does God change his mind? The text does not flinch. The word that is used in English as "repented" in older translations — and in the LXX as **ἐνεθυμήθη** (reflected/considered deeply) and **ἐνεθυμήθη ὁ θεός** — is the Hebrew narrator's direct statement about the interior life of God. God *regretted*. Not metaphorically. Not anthropomorphically disclaimed. He regretted.

The tension with passages like Numbers 23:19 ("God is not a human being, that he should lie, or a son of man, that he should change his mind [nacham]") is a real one, and later theologians (Aquinas, Calvin) worked hard to explain it away. The Hebrew does not explain it away. It says: the LORD regretted.

**וַיִּתְעַצֵּב אֶל־לִבּוֹ (vayyit'atsev el-libbo)** — "it grieved him to his heart." The verb עָצַב (atsav) means to be pained, to grieve, to be wounded in spirit. It is the same root used in Genesis 3:16 and 3:17 — the pain of childbirth, the painful toil of the ground — the twin griefs that followed the garden. Now God experiences it. The pain of Eden has come back around to its Maker.

**אֶל־לִבּוֹ (el-libbo)** — "to his heart." The preposition אֶל (el) suggests direction, movement: the grief moved *into* him, settled *at* or *against* his heart. God does not feel this abstractly. The emotion is located. It is cardiac.

My rendering — "It grieved him to his heart" — attempts to honor the direction the Hebrew gives the grief. This is not a calm divine decision. This is a wound.

---

### Verse 7 — "I will wipe out"

**אֶמְחֶה (emcheh)** — "I will wipe out/blot out/erase." The verb מָחָה (machah) means to wipe, to blot, to erase — the way one wipes a surface clean, or wipes away tears, or erases writing. The same verb is used when God says he will wipe out Israel's sin (Isaiah 43:25, 44:22). The image is violent and total: what was written will be unwritten. What was made will be unmade.

The list — human beings, animals, crawling creatures, birds of the sky — echoes the creation sequence of Genesis 1 in reverse. God is proposing to undo what he made, in something like the order he made it. The flood will be an un-creation.

**כִּי נִחַמְתִּי כִּי עֲשִׂיתִם (ki nichamti ki asitim)** — "for I regret that I made them." The word *nacham* appears again. First it described God's interior state (he was grieved). Now it becomes the stated reason for the decree: regret is the ground of the judgment. This is not divine rage. It is divine sorrow that has reached a breaking point.

---

### Verse 8 — The hinge of the universe

וְנֹחַ מָצָא חֵן בְּעֵינֵי יְהוָה

(*ve-Noach matsa chen be'einei YHWH*)

Seven words. Seven Hebrew words that hold everything together.

**חֵן (chen)** — "favor/grace." This is the first occurrence of the word חֵן in the Hebrew Bible. It means favor, acceptance, grace — what is given freely by the one with power to one without it. Noah did not earn this. The verb is מָצָא (matsa), "to find" — as if favor were something one could stumble upon, discover lying in the road. "Noah *found* favor." The initiative is grammatically Noah's — he found it — but the grace was already there in God's eyes before Noah looked.

The extraordinary placement of this verse is a structural statement. Verse 7: destruction. Verse 8: grace. No transition. No explanation. No rehearsal of Noah's virtues yet — that comes in verse 9. Before we know anything about Noah's character, we know that he found favor. The grace precedes the description of the one who receives it. This is not coincidental.

The verse stands alone — visually isolated from what precedes and what follows — because it is what it is: a shaft of light in the dark. In the architecture of the chapter, it functions the way a window functions in a sealed room.

---

### Verse 9 — Noah: three words, three attributes

נֹחַ אִישׁ צַדִּיק תָּמִים הָיָה בְּדֹרֹתָיו אֶת־הָאֱלֹהִים הִתְהַלֶּךְ־נֹחַ

(*Noach ish tzaddiq tamim hayah bedorotav et-ha'elohim hithalekh-Noach*)

**צַדִּיק (tzaddiq)** — "righteous." The word refers to right conduct in relationship — to being in right-standing with others, with the community, with God. It is a relational, legal, and moral category simultaneously. The righteous person is one who is in proper alignment with the demands of covenant and community.

**תָּמִים (tamim)** — "blameless/complete/whole." From תָּמַם (tamam), to be complete, finished, whole. The word describes integrity — not sinless perfection, but consistency, wholeness, a person whose inside and outside match. It is used of animals that are "without blemish" for sacrifice (Leviticus 1:3), and of God himself: "his ways are tamim" (Deuteronomy 32:4). A tamim person is undivided.

**הִתְהַלֶּךְ (hithalekh)** — "he walked." The Hithpael form of הָלַךְ (halakh) — reflexive/repetitive: he *kept walking*, he *walked habitually*, he *walked continuously*. This is the same form used of Enoch in 5:22 and 5:24 — the only other person in Genesis described this way. The verb's stem puts the emphasis on the ongoing, habitual, intimate nature of the walk. This is not a one-time act of obedience. Noah's life was characterized by a continuous movement *with* God — the two of them going in the same direction together.

The deliberate echo of Enoch is impossible to miss. Enoch walked with God and was taken. Noah walks with God and is saved. Both men stand against the grain of their generations. Noah's righteousness is explicitly contextualized: *among the people of his time* (בְּדֹרֹתָיו, *bedorotav*). This is not universal sainthood. This is faithfulness within a specific, devastating moment.

**בְּדֹרֹתָיו (bedorotav)** — "among the people of his time / in his generations." The plural "generations" is interesting: it may suggest that Noah's faithfulness stretched across multiple generations, or it may simply be the standard Hebrew construct for "his contemporaries." Some rabbis read this phrase critically: Noah was righteous *only* for his time — by Abraham's standards he would have been ordinary. Others read it admiringly: Noah managed faithfulness in the worst possible historical moment. The text does not adjudicate.

---

### Verses 11–12 — "Corrupt" and "violence"

**שָׁחַת (shachat)** — "corrupt/corrupted/ruined." The verb appears three times in close succession (verses 11, 12, 12) — the earth was corrupted before God, God saw the earth and it was corrupted, all flesh had corrupted their way. The repetition is legal, almost formulaic: the indictment is being stated three times as if for the record. The verb means to be destroyed, spoiled, ruined — the same word will be used for what the flood itself does: it "destroys" the earth. Corruption is already the flood, before the flood falls.

**חָמָס (chamas)** — "violence." This word is precise: it refers to violent wrongdoing, injustice enforced by power, the violation of others through force. It is not abstract moral failure — it is physical, social, relational assault. The earth is *filled* (מָלְאָה, *male'ah*) with it — saturated to capacity. The image is of a vessel that can hold no more.

The double diagnosis — corruption and violence — corresponds to the two directions of sin: against God (corruption of "way") and against neighbor (violence). Both axes of the covenant have been broken simultaneously.

---

### Verse 13 — "The end of all flesh has come before me"

**קֵץ כָּל־בָּשָׂר (qets kol-basar)** — "the end of all flesh." The word קֵץ (qets) means end, limit, termination. It is the same word used in prophetic literature for the "end time" — Daniel's visions speak of the qets, the appointed end. Here, the end has arrived at God's presence: it has *come before me*, presented itself, demanded resolution. The phrase is almost legal: the case has been presented; judgment will now be rendered.

---

### Verses 14–16 — The ark: technical specifications and their meaning

The shift in register here is dramatic and intentional. The chapter moves from cosmic tragedy to carpentry specifications. The effect is not bathos — it is anchoring. In the midst of the un-creation, there is a blueprint. Someone is going to build something.

**תֵּבַת עֲצֵי־גֹפֶר (tevat atserei-gofer)** — "an ark of cypress wood" (literally "gopher wood"). The word תֵּבָה (tevah) is used only twice in the Hebrew Bible: here for Noah's vessel, and in Exodus 2:3 for the basket in which Moses is placed in the Nile. Both are vessels of salvation carrying the hope of a people through water. The word is not the ordinary word for a boat or ship (which would be אֳנִיָּה, *oniyyah*). It is a container, a chest, a box.

**גֹּפֶר (gofer)** — the exact species is unknown. Ancient translators guessed: the LXX renders it **ξύλα τετράγωνα** — "squared timbers" — not a species at all. The Vulgate reads *lignis laevigatis*, "smoothed timbers." My rendering "cypress" follows a common traditional interpretation; "cedar" and "acacia" have also been proposed. The text may have used a technical term for a specific timber that is simply lost to us.

**כֹּפֶר (kofer)** — "pitch." Interestingly, this word in Hebrew also means "atonement price," "ransom" — the same root as כִּפֻּרִים (kippurim), the Day of Atonement. The ark is covered inside and out with the material of ransom. The vessel of salvation is sealed with the word for covering-over. This may be accident. It may not be.

The dimensions — 300 cubits × 50 cubits × 30 cubits — produce a vessel with a displacement and proportional ratio (length-to-width of 6:1) that would, if applied to a structure of that scale, produce something extraordinarily stable in heavy seas. Ancient naval architects use similar proportions. Whether the narrator intended this or simply recorded traditional figures, the specification works.

The three decks echo the three-tiered cosmology of the ancient world: heaven, earth, and the waters below. The ark is a microcosm of the created order, preserving within it what the flood will otherwise erase.

---

### Verse 17 — "the breath of life"

**רוּחַ חַיִּים (ruach chayyim)** — "the breath/spirit of life." The phrase echoes 2:7, where God breathed the נִשְׁמַת חַיִּים (nishmat chayyim, "breath of life") into the human form and it became a living being. The word here is רוּחַ (ruach) — breath, wind, spirit — the same word used of the divine *ruach* hovering over the waters at creation (1:2). What God animated at the beginning will be extinguished in the flood — except for what enters the ark. The flood is explicitly an undoing of the gift of breath.

---

### Verse 18 — The first "covenant" in the Bible

**בְּרִיתִי (beriti)** — "my covenant." This is the first occurrence of the word בְּרִית (berit), "covenant," in the Hebrew Bible. God does not explain the covenant's content in full here — that will come in chapter 9, after the flood. Here it is announced: *I will establish my covenant with you.* The unilateral divine commitment precedes everything. God binds himself to Noah before Noah enters the ark, before the rain falls, before anything is survived. The covenant is not the reward for surviving. It is the promise that makes survival conceivable.

The LXX renders: **καὶ στήσω τὴν διαθήκην μου πρὸς σέ** — "and I will establish my covenant with you." The Greek διαθήκη (diatheke) is the word the New Testament will use for both "covenant" and "testament/will" — the word whose use in Hebrews, Galatians, and Luke's Last Supper account builds on this very moment in the narrative chain.

---

### Verse 22 — The obedience of Noah

וַיַּעַשׂ נֹחַ כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים כֵּן עָשָׂה

(*vayyaas Noach kekhol asher tzivvah oto Elohim ken asah*)

Literally: "And Noah did according to all that God commanded him — thus he did."

The doubling is not accidental. The sentence says the same thing twice: *he did all that God commanded*, then *thus he did.* The repetition is emphasis through structure — a kind of narrative underlining. Everything God described in the preceding verses — the precise dimensions, the rooms, the pitch, the roof, the doors, the three decks, the animals, the food — Noah did.

My rendering preserves both clauses: "And Noah did everything — exactly as God commanded him. He did it." The final three words are spare, final, and complete. The chapter ends not with flood or fear but with a human being in motion, obeying, building, doing. The catastrophe is coming. The ark is being built. The one man who found grace is doing his part.

This verse echoes the end of Exodus 39–40, where the construction of the tabernacle is completed with a nearly identical formula: "The Israelites had done all the work just as the LORD had commanded Moses" (39:42). The ark and the tabernacle are structurally parallel: both are divine blueprints executed by faithful builders, both are spaces where the holy and the human meet inside the chaos.

---

### Summary table — key translation choices

| Verse | Hebrew | PNEUMA rendering | LXX (Vaticanus) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 6:2 | בְּנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים | "sons of God" | οἱ ἄγγελοι τοῦ θεοῦ ("angels of God") | LXX makes the supernatural reading explicit; MT preserves ambiguity. |
| 6:3 | לֹא־יָדוֹן רוּחִי | "My spirit will not contend" | "shall not remain" | Hapax legomenon; LXX reads "remain/abide"; "contend" follows MT tradition. |
| 6:3 | מֵאָה וְעֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה | "one hundred and twenty years" | unchanged | Countdown to flood or new lifespan limit — text does not decide. |
| 6:4 | הַנְּפִלִים | "the Nephilim" | γίγαντες ("giants") | LXX interprets; MT preserves the obscure term. DSS expands at length in Book of Giants. |
| 6:4 | אַנְשֵׁי הַשֵּׁם | "men of renown" | ἄνθρωποι ὀνομαστοί ("famous men") | Culture-heroes, legendary figures — the demigods of myth. |
| 6:5 | יֵצֶר מַחְשְׁבֹת לִבּוֹ רַק רָע כָּל־הַיּוֹם | "every inclination... only evil, all the time" | LXX adds "from youth" | Absolute indictment; LXX harmonizes with 8:21; MT keeps the two distinct. |
| 6:6 | וַיִּנָּחֶם יְהוָה | "the LORD regretted" | ἐνεθυμήθη ὁ θεός ("God reflected/considered deeply") | LXX softens; MT is direct and undefended. God regretted. |
| 6:6 | וַיִּתְעַצֵּב אֶל־לִבּוֹ | "it grieved him to his heart" | ἐθυμώθη ("was angered") | LXX shifts to anger; MT has cardiac grief — a wound moving inward. |
| 6:8 | חֵן (chen) | "favor" | χάριν ("grace/favor") | First occurrence of *chen* in the Hebrew Bible; grace precedes merit in the narrative order. |
| 6:9 | צַדִּיק תָּמִים | "righteous... blameless" | δίκαιος τέλειος | תָּמִים = whole, undivided, without blemish — integrity as completeness. |
| 6:9 | הִתְהַלֶּךְ אֶת־הָאֱלֹהִים | "walked with God" | εὐηρέστησεν τῷ θεῷ ("was pleasing to God") | LXX prefers "pleasing" as it did with Enoch; MT prefers the intimacy of walking-together. |
| 6:14 | תֵּבָה | "ark" | κιβωτόν ("chest/box/ark") | Same word as Moses' basket (Exod 2:3); not a ship — a vessel of salvation. |
| 6:14 | כֹּפֶר | "pitch" | ἄσφαλτον ("asphalt/bitumen") | Hebrew *kofer* also means "ransom/atonement" — the ark is sealed with the word for covering. |
| 6:17 | רוּחַ חַיִּים | "the breath of life" | πνοὴν ζωῆς | Echoes 2:7; the breath God gave at creation will be extinguished — except within the ark. |
| 6:18 | בְּרִיתִי | "my covenant" | τὴν διαθήκην μου | First occurrence of *berit* in the Hebrew Bible; the covenant precedes survival, not rewards it. |
| 6:22 | כֵּן עָשָׂה | "He did it" | οὕτως ἐποίησεν | Double emphasis: the obedience is complete, full, without remainder. |

---

### A translator's note on this chapter's emotional register

Genesis 6 requires the translator to hold two emotional temperatures simultaneously without letting either dissolve the other.

The first temperature is the cold, mythological eeriness of verses 1–4: ancient forces, divine beings taking human women, giants walking the earth, an age already half-legendary. This section should feel *old* — archaic, dense, not fully explained, something glimpsed through fog. The translator must not over-explain it. Modernizing it kills it.

The second temperature is the devastating interiority of verses 5–7: the slow accumulation of wickedness, the total indictment, and then — most devastating of all — God's grief. The LORD regretted. The LORD was grieved to his heart. These are not cold judicial statements. These are the cries of a maker watching what he made come apart. They require the translator to slow down, to let each phrase land, to refuse the safety of abstraction.

Between those two temperatures, verse 8 must be a held breath. "But Noah found favor in the eyes of the LORD." Seven words. One sentence. Everything depends on it. The chapter cannot afford for that verse to be crowded.

Then the shift to verses 14–21 — the dimensions, the wood, the pitch, the decks, the animals — must register as a different kind of weight: the weight of precision, of technical responsibility, of a man being given instructions he must follow exactly if anyone is going to survive. The specifications are not interrupting the theology. They *are* the theology. The God who grieved is the God who blueprints. The God who destroys is the God who preserves. Both things are true at once, and the ark is the evidence.

The chapter ends with one of the most quietly powerful lines in the Bible: Noah did everything God commanded him. He did it.

In the wreckage of everything that was going to come — the waters rising, the world ending, the long patience of creation exhausted — one man was building something. He didn't know when it would rain. He didn't know how long it would take. He just built.

*— PNEUMA*
